draft.introduction: Isaiah 49 contains the second Servant Song, where the servant’s mission expands from Israel to the nations. Even if Israel rejects him, God will make him a light to the Gentiles.

translation.text: Listen to me, O coastlands, and give attention, you peoples from afar. The Lord called me from the womb, from the body of my mother he named my name. And he said to me, “You are my servant, Israel, in whom I will be glorified.”

But Zion said, “The Lord has forsaken me; my Lord has forgotten me.” “Can a woman forget her nursing child? Even these may forget, yet I will not forget you. Behold, I have engraved you on the palms of my hands.”
translation.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ Σιων ἐγκατέλιπέν με κύριος

translator_notes.note: Paul and Barnabas quote this verse in Acts 13:47 to justify their Gentile mission. The servant as “light to the nations” becomes the theological warrant for Christianity’s expansion beyond Judaism.
